title: practical english usage - Part 362
content:
The  expression only too is used to mean ‘ very'', ‘ extremely’ . It is common in 8  only     too    . ..

I'm not feeling too well.

Oh,  that's really too kind of you - thank you so much.

In informal speech too can sometimes be used to mean ‘ very’ .

7  That's      really      too    kind     of   you.

2.  He's too stupid for anyone to teach - he can't be taught.

1.  He's too stupid to be a teacher.

Note the two possible meanings of sentences like He's too stupid to teach: The water is too salty for us to drink (it).

However, object pronouns are possible in structures with for.

The water is loo salty to drink, (     -Phe-water-is too salty to drink it.) after the infinitive in this case.

(For more about this structure, ►     101.4). Object pronouns are not normally used The  subject of a sentence with too can also be the object of a following infinitive.

6 too    salty      to   drink,      etc There was too much  snow for us to go walking.

It's too late for the pubs to be open.

If the infinitive has its own subject, this is introduced by for (►   113).

There was too much  snow to go walking.

We   can also use an infinitive structure after too much/many.

He's too old to work.   It's far too cold to go out.

We   can use an infinitive structure after too + adjective/adverb.

5  too    . . . 4- infinitive It's too cold a day for tennis.

(►   187). Note the word order.

In a rather formal style, too can be used before adjective + a/an + noun not Let's forget this problem - it's too difficult, (     . .. this too difficult problem.) not She doesn't like men who are too tall, (     She doesn't-like-too tall men.) not / put down the bag because it was too heavy, (     ... the too heavy bag.) Too is not normally used before adjective + noun.

4 not   used    before     adjective       + noun Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary «611     too much  and too many are very hard and/or take a very long time.

We   do not so often use trip for expeditions which  have a very serious purpose, ‘ How was your trip? ‘ Successful.’ ‘ How was your journey? ‘ The train broke down.’ Compare: Daniel’s school is organising a skiing trip to the Alps.

going to do some business.) I’m  going on a business trip next week. (= I’ m  going on a journey and I'm is the reason for the journey.

A  trip is a return journey together with the activity (business or pleasure) which not I met Megan on my last Journey to England, (     . . .-my last travel.) not Did you have a good journey? (     Did you have-a good^ravel?) A  journey (more common in British English) is one ‘ piece' of travelling.

He wrote a wonderful book about his travels in the Himalayas.

travelling or exploration.

The  plural travels is sometimes used; it suggests a rather grand programme of My interests are music and travel.

Travel means ‘ travelling in general'. It is normally uncountable.

612 travel,      journey,        trip    and   voyage ►    420 and town         city For die difference between too and too much,  ►     6 I ().2.

‘ Did you get any answers to your advertisement?’ ‘ Too many.’ You’ve eaten too much.

We   can drop a noun after too much/many, if the meaning is clear.

3   too    much/many            without      a  noun not You ask far too many questions, (     .. . much  too many questions?) However, much  too many is unusual.

I’ve been to rather too many parties recently.

She’s wearing a bit too much  make-up for my taste.

too much  and too many.

Expressions which  modify comparatives (►  207) and too can also modify 2 a  bit    too    much,      rather       too    many,      etc not I’ve had too many late nights recently, (     ... too much  late-nights-. ..) You put too much  salt in the soup.

nouns; too many is used with plurals.

between much  and many (►   163). Too much  is used with singular (uncountable) The  difference between too much  and too many is the same as the difference 1  the   difference 611  too    much      and   too    many                  __ vocabulary •  613   until I waited until the rain had stopped.
